w:document.mc:Ignorable: w14 w15 w16se w16cid w16 w16cex w16sdtdh w16sdtfl w16du wp14

w:r.w:t: Security Research Report: TON DNS / Telemint NoDns Contracts
DNS-like NFT assets using auction-based ownership transfer.
The Telemint repository states that NftCollectionNoDns and NftItemNoDns do not support DNS and allow additional restrictions on first bid. This matches the likely design for anonymous numbers, but
on-chain code hash should be compared against production collection/item code before reporting as production-impacting
Finding 1 — Signed first-bid deploy query may be front-runnable if force_sender_address restriction is absent
This finding is valid if a real production/test signed deploy payload has:

NftCollectionNoDns accepts a signed deploy command for the first bid. The signature wrapper validates the server signature, subwallet, and validity window, but the signed command is only bound to the actual sender if the optional restrictions field contains force_sender_address.

If a signed deploy payload is issued without force_sender_address, the payload behaves like a bearer token. Anyone who sees it before execution can submit the same signed body from their own wallet and become the first bidder.
This matches a known TON risk pattern: signed messages should include critical context and replay/front-run protection, because pending messages can be
observed and reused. TON security guidance explicitly discusses signed-message replay/front-running risks.
Attacker can front-run a victim’s first-bid deploy transaction.
Attacker becomes the initial bidder for a private number / NoDns NFT.
Victim’s original transaction may fail, become duplicate, or lose the intended mint/auction slot.
This is especially relevant for rare Telegram numbers or other high-value NoDns assets.
Production signed queries can omit force_sender_address
Production always includes force_sender_address, but contract permits unsafe integration
Signed query also has backend one-time protection unavailable to attacker
The signed deploy command allows sender restriction as an optional field instead of mandatory signed context.
The Telemint README describes NftCollectionNoDns / NftItemNoDns as contracts that allow additional restrictions on first bid, not necessarily mandatory restrictions.
Victim requests a private number / NoDns NFT purchase or first bid.
Backend returns a signed deploy payload.
Attacker observes the pending transaction body.
Attacker submits the same body with sufficient value from their own wallet.
If force_sender_address is absent, collection accepts attacker as actual sender.
NFT item is deployed with attacker as first bidder.
Victim’s transaction arrives later and no longer achieves the intended first bid.
How to verify in VS Code
Check whether the actual sender_address is part of the mandatory signed payload.
sender_address is not mandatory in the signed wrapper
sender check is conditional on restrictions.has_force_sender_address
Decode the body and inspect the final maybe_ref restrictions.
it("allows another sender to reuse signed deploy when force_sender_address is absent", async () => {
// In real front-run, attacker tx lands first.
Make intended bidder address a mandatory signed field.
Reject deploy messages where actual sender_address does not equal the signed intended bidder.
Do not rely on optional restrictions for sender binding.
Include all critical context in the signed hash:
Finding 2 — Malicious auction winner may be able to block auction finalization via failing ownership_assigned notification
Strong candidate. Needs sandbox confirmation of action-phase behavior.

During auction finalization, NftItemNoDns sends an ownership_assigned notification to the winning bidder before completing settlement. The send mode appears to pay fees separately but does not include SendIgnoreErrors (+2).
TON message mode documentation defines +2 as SendIgnoreErrors; without it, send/action errors are not ignored.

If the winning bidder is a malicious contract that causes the outbound notification to fail, finalization may revert or fail in the action phase. This could leave the NFT in an unfinished auction state and prevent beneficiary payout.
Asset remains stuck in auction state.
Anyone trying to finalize repeats the same failure.
High if finalization can be permanently blocked.
Medium if only notification fails but state still updates.
Low / Invalid if TVM/action semantics do not revert this path.
Settlement performs an external notification to the winner before final state/payout settlement, and the notification is sent without ignore_errors.
In TON, send modes matter: +2 is the flag for ignoring errors during message sending.
Item contract sends ownership_assigned to BadBidder.
BadBidder rejects/fails or otherwise causes send/action failure.
() recv_internal(int msg_value, cell in_msg_full, slice in_msg_body) impure {
Depending on exact bounce/action semantics, a stronger version may be needed:
() recv_internal(int msg_value, cell in_msg_full, slice in_msg_body) impure {
or a contract with code/data/state that creates an action-phase failure on delivery.
Look for a send to bidder:
Mode 1 means pay forward fees separately. It does
it("malicious winner can block auction finalization", async () => {
If transaction success is true, still check:
If state updates despite notification failure, the finding is invalid or downgraded.
future finalization attempts fail the same way
Send ownership_assigned notification with SendIgnoreErrors, i.e. mode including +2.
Complete state transition before non-critical notification.
Ensure seller/beneficiary payout cannot be blocked by winner receiver behavior.
Finding 3 — DNS / TON domain auction refund DoS candidate
Candidate. Lower priority than Finding 2. Needs sandbox test.
TON DNS repo documents that nft-item.fc implements the domain NFT item and contains auction functionality.

The TON DNS domain auction refunds the previous highest bidder when a new higher bid arrives. If the refund message is sent without ignore_errors, a malicious previous bidder may be able to block future bids by making refund delivery fail.
Auction can be locked by malicious highest bidder.
Domain auction is griefed or stuck until timeout/finalization.
Valuable domains may be captured or settlement can be manipulated.
High if malicious previous bidder can block all outbids.
Medium if only partial griefing / temporary DoS.
Low / Invalid if refund failure does not revert state.
Malicious contract places current max bid.
send refund with mode including +2 SendIgnoreErrors
Finding 4 — DNS record parser/client desync via unvalidated DNS record cells
Research candidate. Requires multi-client differential testing.
DNS item owner can set arbitrary record cells for DNS categories. The contract stores the value but does not deeply validate every TL-B schema for wallet/site/storage/resolver records.
This may be intended at contract level, but it creates a cross-client attack surface: wallets, explorers, resolvers, and SDKs may parse malformed cells differently.
Wallet resolves a .ton domain to a different address than another client.
TON Sites / ADNL resolution crashes or follows malicious delegation.
Client-side DoS or phishing via malformed DNS records.
Medium / High if wallet payment resolution can be desynced.
Low / Medium if only UI crash.
Wallets should reject malformed records instead of best-effort parsing.
Contract-level validation could be added for high-risk categories, if backward-compatible.
Suggested test task list for VS Code agent
Task A — Confirm Finding 1 from code
3. Verify whether sender_address is included in mandatory signed hash.
- Is signed deploy sender-bound by default?
- Is sender binding only optional via restrictions?
Task B — Confirm Finding 1 from real payload
1. Obtain real in_msg body for op 0x4637289b.
2. Make BadBidder highest bidder in NftItemNoDns auction.
if finalization fails and auction remains active:
elif owner/payout succeeds despite failed notification:
Task D — Test DNS refund DoS
Best if sandbox confirms. It is pure on-chain and does not depend on backend config.
Signed deploy front-run when force-sender absent
Best if you obtain one real payload without force_sender_address.
Similar class to Finding 2, but needs confirmation.
Useful if differential test finds real wallet/explorer inconsistency.
I reviewed Telemint NoDns and TON DNS auction flows.

1. NftCollectionNoDns signed first-bid deploy payloads are only sender-bound if optional force_sender_address restrictions are present. If production signed payloads omit this restriction, the signed deploy body can be front-run/reused by another wallet to become the first bidder.

2. NftItemNoDns auction finalization sends ownership_assigned to the winning bidder with send mode 1, without SendIgnoreErrors (+2), before settlement completes. A malicious winner contract may be able to make finalization fail repeatedly, leaving the auction unresolved and blocking payout.
Both issues should be verified with sandbox tests and, for issue #1, with a real signed deploy payload.
